title: Concurrency Deep Dive
summary: Happens-before ordering, volatile, atomic classes, locks, and the Java Memory Model foundations of safe concurrency.

sections.title: CPU Cache Coherence Problem
sections.content:
graph LR
    subgraph CPU1
        C1[L1 Cache\nvalue=42]
    end
    subgraph CPU2
        C2[L1 Cache\nvalue=0]
    end
    RAM[Main Memory\nvalue=0]
    CPU1 -->|not yet flushed| RAM
    CPU2 -->|reads stale| RAM
    style C1 fill:#059669,color:#fff
    style C2 fill:#dc2626,color:#fff
    style RAM fill:#4f46e5,color:#fff

## Happens-Before

The **happens-before** relationship guarantees that memory writes by one specific statement are visible to reads by another. Key rules:

- **Program order rule** — each action in a thread happens-before every action later in that thread
- **Monitor lock rule** — `unlock` of a monitor happens-before every subsequent `lock` of that monitor
- **Volatile variable rule** — write to volatile happens-before every subsequent read of that variable
- **Thread start rule** — `Thread.start()` happens-before any action in the started thread
- **Thread termination rule** — any action in thread happens-before `Thread.join()` returns
- **Transitivity** — if A happens-before B, and B happens-before C, then A happens-before C

sections.title: Happens-Before Chain
sections.content:
graph LR
    W[Thread 1: write x=42] -->|happens-before| V[Thread 1: volatile write flag=true]
    V -->|happens-before via volatile| R[Thread 2: volatile read flag]
    R -->|happens-before| RX[Thread 2: read x]
    RX --> Result[sees x=42 guaranteed]
    style W fill:#f59e0b,color:#fff
    style V fill:#4f46e5,color:#fff
    style Result fill:#059669,color:#fff

## volatile

Declaring a field `volatile` provides two guarantees:
1. **Visibility** — reads always see the latest write from any thread
2. **No reordering** — reads/writes cannot be reordered relative to other volatile accesses

```java
private volatile boolean ready = false;

// Now safe: write to value happens-before write to ready,
// and any thread reading ready=true will also see value=42
```

**Limitations of volatile:**
- Does NOT make compound actions atomic (`i++` is still not thread-safe)
- Use for flags, status, single-writer scenarios
- Use `AtomicInteger` / `synchronized` for compound operations

sections.title: CAS (Compare-And-Swap) Operation
sections.content:
sequenceDiagram
    participant T1 as Thread 1
    participant Mem as Memory (x=5)
    participant T2 as Thread 2
    T1->>Mem: Read x=5
    T2->>Mem: Read x=5
    T1->>Mem: CAS(5, 6) → SUCCESS, x=6
    T2->>Mem: CAS(5, 6) → FAIL (x is now 6)
    T2->>Mem: Retry: Read x=6
    T2->>Mem: CAS(6, 7) → SUCCESS, x=7

sections.title: Always release locks in finally blocks
sections.content: If an exception is thrown inside a lock block without a finally, the lock will never be released, causing deadlock. Always use: lock.lock(); try { ... } finally { lock.unlock(); }

sections.question: Which of the following makes `i++` thread-safe on a shared counter?
options: Using i inside a try-finally block
sections.explanation: i++ is a read-modify-write compound operation (not atomic). volatile only guarantees visibility of single reads/writes. AtomicInteger.incrementAndGet() uses CAS to atomically read and increment, making it thread-safe without locks.
